coredata.dc:title: Enhancement anti-interference ability of photoelectrochemical sensor via differential molecularly imprinting technique demonstrated by dopamine determination
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Qi
Zhang, Qiao
Zang, Lixin
Zhao, Mengru
Chen, Xiaolan
Shen, Dazhong
coredata.dc:description: Molecularly imprinting polymers (MIPs), as artificial antibodies with high recognition selectivity to template molecules, are widely used in various biosensors. To improve further the selectivity of MIPs-based photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensors, we report a differential strategy using non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) as the reference. In a proof-to-concept example for the determination of dopamine (DA), MIPs and NIPs membranes were fabricated by electrochemical polymerization of polypyrrole membranes on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/TiO2 nanotubes (NTs). The photocurrent difference between the two PEC cells, MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt and NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, was measured as the signal. As the non-specific adsorption of non-template molecules on the outside surface of MIPs and NIPs membranes is similar, the anti-interference ability for the determination of DA is much improved by using differential strategy. In the normal and differential PEC measurement models, 10.0 μM ascorbic acid is equivalent to 3.12 and 0.40 μM DA, respectively. Further, the smaller specific surface area in NIPs membrane was compensated by using a weight factor to correct the residual interference in a modified differential model. By using 10.0 μM ascorbic acid as the balance point, the presence of 10.0 μM H2O2, glutathione, uric acid or glucose is equivalent only to 0.090, 0.061,0.11 or 0.041 μM of DA, respectively, which are about 3–7% of their interference levels in the normal photocurrent model. The differential PEC method was applied in the determination of DA in serum samples in the linear range of 0.05–12.5 μM, with the detection limit of 0.018 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical sensor
Molecular imprinting polymer
Differential measurement
Polypyrrole
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the MIPs-based DA PEC sensing platform
Differential PEC sensor for DA determination
Characterization of MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs and NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs
PEC responses of the TiO2 NTs based photoelectrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The reagents used are analytical pure or better grade. Ultrapure water (specific resistance >18 MΩ cm) was used throughout in the experiments. Titanium sheet (purity 99.5%, thickness 0.5 mm), ascorbic acid (AA), pyrrole, glucose (Glu), glutathione (GSH), and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Company Ltd (China). Citric acid, ethylene glycol, ammonium fluoride, lithium perchlorate, and disodium hydrogen phosphate were purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Company Ltd. Unless otherwise specified, Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) buffer of 0.1 M pH 7.0 was used as the supporting electrolyte.This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874083, 61905132 and 21575080) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR2018MB029).
This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21874083 , 61905132 and 21575080 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China ( ZR2018MB029 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images TiO2 NTs(A),GQDs/TiO2 NTs (B), NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs(c), MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs.

The chopped response curves of different photoelectrodes in two electrode system. (a) TiO2 NTs−Pt, (b) GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, (c) NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, (d) (DA)MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs −Pt, (e) MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs −Pt.

The chopped response curves of the photovoltage and inner resistance (B) of the photocells. (a) GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, (b) (DA)MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, (c) MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt. The solid and hollow arrows indicate the on and off states of the shutter, respectively.
Effect of (A) molar ratio of DA to pyrrole, (B) elution time, (C) response time, and (D) pH on the PEC response of MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs photoelectrode to 10.0 μM DA.
Photocurrent and differential photocurrent responses to DA. (a) ΔIMIPs, (b) ΔINIPs, (c) ΔIMIPs −ΔINIPs, (d) ΔIMIPs −1.13ΔINIPs.
Photocurrent responses and photocurrent difference between sensing and reference photoelectrodes to some interferents. The concentrations of DA and interferents were 10.0 μM.
Schematic of the preparation of NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs and MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs as the reference and sensing photoelectrodes in the differential PEC method for DA determination.
Comparison of analytical performance of some PEC sensors for dopamine determination.
Recovery and RSD for DA determination in the spiked serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Enhancement anti-interference ability of photoelectrochemical sensor via differential molecularly imprinting technique demonstrated by dopamine determination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Key Laboratory of Molecular and Nano Probes, Ministry of Education, Shandong Normal University, Jinan, 250014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Molecular and Nano Probes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Key Laboratory of Molecular and Nano Probes, Ministry of Education, Shandong Normal University, Jinan, 250014, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecularly imprinting polymers (MIPs), as artificial antibodies with high recognition selectivity to template molecules, are widely used in various biosensors. To improve further the selectivity of MIPs-based photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensors, we report a differential strategy using non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) as the reference. In a proof-to-concept example for the determination of dopamine (DA), MIPs and NIPs membranes were fabricated by electrochemical polymerization of polypyrrole membranes on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/TiO2 nanotubes (NTs). The photocurrent difference between the two PEC cells, MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt and NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, was measured as the signal. As the non-specific adsorption of non-template molecules on the outside surface of MIPs and NIPs membranes is similar, the anti-interference ability for the determination of DA is much improved by using differential strategy. In the normal and differential PEC measurement models, 10.0 μM ascorbic acid is equivalent to 3.12 and 0.40 μM DA, respectively. Further, the smaller specific surface area in NIPs membrane was compensated by using a weight factor to correct the residual interference in a modified differential model. By using 10.0 μM ascorbic acid as the balance point, the presence of 10.0 μM H2O2, glutathione, uric acid or glucose is equivalent only to 0.090, 0.061,0.11 or 0.041 μM of DA, respectively, which are about 3–7% of their interference levels in the normal photocurrent model. The differential PEC method was applied in the determination of DA in serum samples in the linear range of 0.05–12.5 μM, with the detection limit of 0.018 μM.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensors have been attracted the increasing attention resulting from their fascinating features of low cost and simple instrumentation, high sensitivity, and easier miniaturization [1–3]. The photoactive materials in PEC sensors are excited by photons to produce electrons and holes, and the photocurrent is recorded as the analytical signal. But the photocatalytic oxidation may be lack of enough selectivity due to the strong reaction activity of the photogenerated holes or hydroxyl radicals. To improve the selectivity of PEC methods, the probes with excellent recognition selectivity, such as enzymes, antibodies, nucleic acids, and aptamers are widely used in fabrication of various PEC biosensors [4–6]. It should be noted that these biological receptors are usually costly and their structure stability is sensitive to surrounding conditions. Recently, numerous attempts have been made to replace the biological receptors with synthetic counterparts as the recognition elements in chemo/biosensors. Of various approaches, molecular imprinting technique has become a powerful tool to prepare the polymeric materials with selective recognition to the target species [7–10]. Hence, molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) have been widely used in industrial and environmental applications [11,12], sample treatments [13,14], chromatographic separation [15], and recognition elements in chemical or biological sensing [16–18]. Researchers have paid considerable attentions to amalgamate MIPs recognition elements with PEC sensing devices [19–23]. By using MIPs membranes as the recognition elements, PEC sensors exhibit excellent sensitivity, selectively and applicability.

Ideally, MIPs should possess selective biomimetic recognition ability to the target molecules, providing interesting substitutes to biological receptors. But by now, MIPs as artificially synthetic functional materials are still inferior to their biological rivals (antibodies etc.) in recognition selectivity. The recognition selectivity of MIPs can be evaluated by the imprinting factor (IF), which is defined as the ratio of the adsorption capacities of MIPs to non-imprinted polymers (NIPs). Due to the non-specific adsorption on the outside surface of MIPs particles, the IF values are usually in the range of 2–10 [24–27] and only a few are higher than 15 [28–30], implying that the selectivity of the MIPs based sensors is still not high enough for the applications in complicated samples. In most of the MIPs researches, NIPs were prepared merely for the purpose to calculate the IF values. It is worth mentioning that the amounts of non-template molecules adsorbed onto the MIPs and NIPs particles (prepared in same material) are similar in lots of systems [15,31–33]. Hence, the responses of MIPs and NIPs to these non-template molecules are usually similar in the MIPs-based sensors, including fluorescence [29,34–37], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [38], electrochemical [39–41], quartz crystal microbalance [42], and PEC [43], etc.

Inspire by the aforementioned results, the anti-interference ability of MIPs-based sensors is expected to enhance by using a differential strategy, subtracting the interference from the non-specific adsorption on the outside surface of the MIPs according to the response of the NIPs reference channel. In this work, we report a proof-of-concept example of such differential strategy, using MIPs-based PEC dopamine (DA) sensor as the model. DA as one of the central neurotransmitters, plays important roles in many physiological and pathological processes [44]. MIPs electrochemical sensors for DA determination were reported [41,45]. Scheme 1 shows the schematic illustration of the differential MIPs-based PEC method. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/TiO2 nanotubes (NTs) based photoelectrode was used because TiO2 NTs are nontoxic, biocompatible, photocorrosion free, and cost-effective PEC materials [46]. The synergistic effect between GQDs and TiO2 NTs in the GQDs/TiO2 NTs can enhance the photocurrent significantly [47]. The high photocurrent level in GQDs/TiO2 NTs offers the convenience to perform the differential measurement in a simplified two-electrode system. In this work, polypyrrole (PPy) membrane was grown on the surface of GQDs/TiO2 NTs by electropolymerization, which is an easy way in the preparation and control of film thickness and morphology. The electropolymerized membranes have a strong adherence to the transducer and a rapid response to template molecules. The high specific surface area of GQDs/TiO2 NTs provides more multidimensional space for the growth of PPy membrane. The photocurrent difference between the sensing PEC channel of MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt and reference PEC channel of NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt were measured as the signal indicator. In a modified differential PEC method, a weight factor (α = 1.13) is used in the NIPs channel to compensate the smaller specific surface area in NIPs membrane, which enhances significantly the anti-interference ability. In addition, the differential strategy also helpful to eliminate the baseline drift in the PEC sensor. The modified differential strategy is expected to be suitable in other optical and electrochemical MIPs-based sensors.

The reagents used are analytical pure or better grade. Ultrapure water (specific resistance >18 MΩ cm) was used throughout in the experiments. Titanium sheet (purity 99.5%, thickness 0.5 mm), ascorbic acid (AA), pyrrole, glucose (Glu), glutathione (GSH), and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Company Ltd (China). Citric acid, ethylene glycol, ammonium fluoride, lithium perchlorate, and disodium hydrogen phosphate were purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Company Ltd. Unless otherwise specified, Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) buffer of 0.1 M pH 7.0 was used as the supporting electrolyte.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) was used to characterize the morphologies of MIPs and NIPs membranes. The homemade differential PEC experimental setup is similar to that used in a previous paper [48], the schematic drawing is demonstrated in Fig. S1 in Electronic Supporting Materials (ESM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the MIPs-based DA PEC sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs were prepared according to the protocol in previous reports [49] with some modifications, the details are described in Experimental S1 in the ESM. TiO2 NTs were prepared by electrochemical anodization of Ti foil in a fluorine-containing electrolyte according the procedure described in detail previously [50]. 1.0 mg of GQDs was ultrasonically dispersed in 1.0 mL ultrapure water. Then 50 μL of the as-obtained dispersion was spread on the surface of TiO2 NTs electrode with the area of 1.5 cm2 by sealing with 704 silicone glue and dried at 150 °C for 30 min, obtained GQDs/TiO2 NTS photoelectrode.

The GQDs/TiO2 NTs electrode was first electrochemically pretreated at potential of −1.5 V in 2 M NH4Cl aqueous electrolytes for 3 s. After rinsed with ultrapure water and dried, GQDs/TiO2 NTs electrode was immersed in aqueous solution containing 0.1 M LiClO4, 0.5 mM pyrrole and 0.1 mM DA. After cyclic potential scanning in the range of −0.2 V and 1.0 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s for 10 cycles, PPy membrane was electropolymerizated onto GQDs/TiO2 NTs. Reference photoelectrode of NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs was prepared in the same process without DA. Then the template molecules of DA were removed from the PPy membrane by electrochemical oxidation at 1.3 V in 0.2 M Na2HPO4 solution for 20 min, obtained the sensing photoelectrode of MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differential PEC sensor for DA determination
ce:section.ce:para:
Under light irradiation of a xenon lamp, the photocurrents from the MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt and MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt channels were measured and recorded by double-channel chromatography data system (N2000, Hangzhou Yijie Technology Co. Ltd., China). In the normal differential model, the photocurrent difference between the MIPs and NIPs channels, ΔI, is calculated by:
(1) ΔI=ΔI MIPs−ΔI NIPs
where ΔI MIPs and ΔI NIPs are the photocurrent changes in MIPs and NIPs channels relative to blank, respectively. In the modified differential model, a weight factor of α is added in the photocurrent of NIPs channel to calculate ΔI by eq. (2).
(2) ΔI=ΔI MIPs−αΔI NIPs
where the value of α was estimation by using 10 μM AA as the balance point (ΔIMIPs = αΔINIPs), which was achieved by adjusting the sample resistance ratio (α = R 0/R) in the sensing and reference channels.

In the determination of DA in human serum samples, the non-pathologic human blood samples were supplied from healthy volunteers at the Qianfoshan Hospital of Shandong Province. The blood samples were allowed to clot, and then centrifuged for 15 min to remove the thrombus, and the resulted serum is stored at −20 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs and NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 A, the morphology of the as-prepared TiO2 NTs photoelectrode is relatively regular. The TiO2 NT array was uniformly and vertically grown on the surface of the titanium sheet with the averaged inside diameter of the nanotubes about 70 nm. The porous structure of TiO2 NTs is favorable for the subsequent surface modifications. In the GQDs/TiO2 NTs, the TiO2 NTs array was well covered by GQDs, the previous regular nanopores become irregular shape with the diameter less than 35 nm (Fig. 1B). After electropolymerization, PPy membrane was deposited on the surface of GQDs/TiO2 NTs (Fig. 1C). Compared with NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs, the surface porosity in MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs (Fig. 1D) is increased slightly due to the template removing by an electrochemical oxidation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC responses of the TiO2 NTs based photoelectrodes
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 , the basic PEC cell of TiO2 NTs−Pt exhibited high photocurrent density, because the emission spectrum of the xenon light source covers ultraviolet and visible light regions. When the light shutter was opened, the photocurrent was increased from background level (<0.05 μA/cm2) to a maximum of 0.181 mA/cm2 in 3 s then decayed to a stable level of 0.127 mA/cm2 in 8 s (curve a). Shutting off the light, the photocurrent was decayed rapidly to the background level in 3 s. In the PEC cell of GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, the steady-state photocurrent was increased to 0.313 mA/cm2 (curve b), which is 2.47 times of that in TiO2 NTs−Pt PEC cell. One reason is that GQDs can absorb visible light and generate additional photocurrent. At the same time, the nanocomposites of GQDs/TiO2 NTs is favorable to separate the photogenerated holes and electrons, enhancing the PEC efficiency [47]. After depositing of PPy membrane, the photocurrent in NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt was increased further to 0.393 mA/cm2 (curve c). This enhancement may result from the conjugation effect in PPy membrane, which will lower the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, accelerating the charge transfer and enhance the photocurrent [51–53].

In the presence of the template molecules of DA, the steady-state photocurrent in (DA)MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt was increased to 1.81 mA/cm2 (curve d), indicating that the template molecule of DA can significantly enhance the photocurrent, because the oxidation product of DA by the photogenerated holes could effectively avoid the electron-hole recombination. Consequently, after removing template molecules of DA, the photocurrent in MIPs@GQDs/TiO2NTs−Pt (curve e) was reduced to the similar level in NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt.

According to the method reported previously [54], the potential differences (ΔE) between the photoelectrodes and Pt electrode in PEC cells were measured and shown in Fig. 3 A. It can be seen that initial ΔE values are about 162, 176 and 171 mV in GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, (DA)MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt and MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, respectively. Under light illumination, the photovoltage of the photoelectrodes was increased rapidly in the initial 5–7 s and then approached to the stable level. After an illumination time of 40 s, the ΔE values were increased to 397, 588 and 545 mV, corresponding to the observed photovoltage of 235, 412 and 374 mV in GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, (DA)MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt and MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, respectively. When the light was shut off, the photovoltage of the photoelectrodes was decayed rapidly but the ΔE values did not returned to the previous initial levels, indicating that a small photovoltage was remained in a short time. It can be seen that the change rate of potential difference is different to that of photocurrent in Fig. 2. According to Ohm’s Law, the inner resistances (R) of the PEC cells were calculated and compared in Fig. 3B. Under the excitation by xenon lamp, the values of R were significantly reduced from several MΩ to minima then approached to the stable levels of 1.28, 0.485 and 1.25 kΩ cm2 in GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, (DA)MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt and MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, respectively. The stable values of photocurrent, ΔE and R −1 in (DA)MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt are about 278%, 111% and 257% of those in MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt, manifesting that the photocurrent response is due mainly to decrease in the inner resistance of the PEC cells as well as the increase in photovoltage.

During the electropolymerization of pyrrole, the template molecules of DA were embedded in the PPy membrane via hydrogen bond interaction, obtained (DA)MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs. Hence, the molar ratio of DA to pyrrole was optimized firstly. As can be seen from Fig. 4 A, the maximum photocurrent response was obtained at the molar ratio of 1:5, which was used as the optimal molar ratio in the fabrication of the MIPs membrane. With increasing elution time, more rebinding sites were available in the MIPs membrane, the photocurrent response to DA was enhanced (Fig. 4B). After an elution time of 30 min, the photocurrent response was approached to stable level, implying that most of the template molecules of DA had been removed. Thus, the elution time of 30 min was chosen in the fabrication of MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs.

To reduce the response time, a sensor should be able to recognize the target analyte as quickly as possible. But the rebinding process was taken place mainly at the interface of the MIPs/solution, resulting in a rather long response time in the MIPs-based PEC sensors. As shown in Fig. 4C, the photocurrent response of the MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs was enhanced with increasing incubation time, as more DA molecules were rebound to the MIPs membrane. After an incubation time of 25 min, the photocurrent was approached to stable level gradually. Compromised between the sensitivity and analytical time, the incubation time of 25 min was used for the determination of DA.

The pH value of solution usually affects the response of PEC sensor in a complicated ways, including the dissociation of DA, surface potential of the electrode and redox reaction at the electrode/solution interface. As depicted in Fig. 4D, the maximum photocurrent response was achieved at pH = 7 in the range of pH 5.5–9 tested. Thus, PBS buffer with pH = 7 was used as the measurement medium in the PEC sensor for DA determination.

Under the aforementioned optimized conditions, the normal calibration curve of MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs to DA is shown in Fig. 5 (curve a). The linear regression equation is expressed as ΔI MIPs (μA/cm2) = 4.08 + 29.12CDA (μM) (r2 = 0.9986) with the linear range of 0.05–12.5 μM. Based on the 3σ rule (σ is the standard deviation in blank), the limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 0.031 μM. On the other hand, NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs also exhibits an inferior photocurrent response to DA (curve b). According to the slope ratio of MIPs to NIPs, the IF value MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs to DA is estimated to be 4.66, indicating that the as-prepared MIPs membrane has higher adsorption capacity to DA than NIPs membrane.

Specificity is an essential criterion for the performance of biosensors. From the perspective of practical application, the IF value of 4.66 is not high enough to obtain good selectivity for DA determination in complicated samples. As shown in Fig. 6 , the photocurrent of MIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs−Pt was also enhanced in the presence of 10.0 μM AA, H2O2, UA, GSH or Glu, which is equivalent to 3.12, 2.51, 1.97, 1.53 or 0.62 μM DA, implying the serious interference from these non-target molecules for the determination of DA. Among them, AA is shown the most serious interference because AA is an excellent scavenger for the photogenerated holes at the photoelectrode/solution interface. Although the cavities imprinting for DA in MIPs membrane are not matched with the size and shape of AA, the redox reaction between photogenerated holes and AA can still been taken place at the outside surface of the MIPs, because PPy is a conductive polymer. Significantly, the photocurrent responses of NIPs@GQDs/TiO2 NTs to the five interferents are similar to those of MIPs@ GQDs/TiO2 NTs, which offers the possibility to preform differential measurements. As shown in Fig. 5, the differential photocurrent response (ΔI MIPs−ΔI NIPs) is in good linear correlation with the concentration of DA (curve c). Importantly, most of the interferences were suppressed in the differential model (Fig. 6). For example, 10.0 μM AA, H2O2, UA, GSH or Gluis is equivalent only to 0.40, 0.43, 0.34,0.32 or 0.13 μM DA in the differential model, respectively, which is about 12.7%–21.2% of the interference levels in normal photocurrent model.

It is worth noting that the necessary condition to perform a successful differential strategy in MIPs-based sensors is that the responses of the interferents on the MIPs and NIPs channels should be very similar, which is true in many of the MIPs-based sensors [15,29,31–43]. But there are still a few examples with rather large difference in the responses between MIPs and NIPs [55–57], which reduces the efficiency of differential strategy. In fact, as shown in Fig. 6, the responses of the NIPs channel to the five interferents tested are less slightly than those of the MIPs channel, which may be due to the smoother surface in the NIPs membrane. As a result, about 12.7%–21.2% of the interference levels are still residual in the differential model. To improve further the anti-interference ability of the MIPs-based PEC sensor, a weigh factor of α (>1) is added in the response from NIPs channel in modified differential strategy. Consequently, eq. (2) offers better applicability than eq. (1), especially in the systems with rather large difference in the responses between MIP and NIP channels.

In the modified differential method, the species with most serious interfere in the sample should be used to estimate the weigh factor of α, maximizing the anti-interference ability of the MIPs-based sensor. In this work, the 10 μM ascorbic acid solution is used as balance point (ΔIMIPs = αΔINIPs) for two reasons. One is that AA is an excellent scavenger for photogenerated hole in lots of PEC sensors. Another is that AA is a major interference for DA determination in serum sample by the proposed PEC sensor. Under the experimental conditions, the interference from AA is corrected completely by using α = 1.13 (Fig. 6). The value of α can be used to adjust the sample resistance ratio of R 0/R in the differential PEC device in Fig. S1 or the calculation of ΔI in eq. (2) if R 0/R = 1. Significantly, besides the primary interferent (AA), the interference from other species is also much reduced. For example, the presence of 10 μM H2O2, UA, GSH or Glu is equivalent only to 0.090, 0.11, 0.061, or 0.041 μM of DA, respectively, which are about 3–7% of their interference levels in normal photocurrent model, demonstrating the excellent anti-interference ability in the modified differential PEC method. Comparing with the normal PEC measurement model, however, two PEC cells are needed in the differential approach, which complicates the experimental operation and data treatments, especially in the modified differential method. On the other hand, if the photocurrent in two-electrode system is measured by chromatography data system (or multimeter), the TiO2 NTs based or other photoelectrodes with high photocurrent density are preferred.

It can be seen from Fig. 5 that the differential photocurrent signal also has a linear relationship with the concentration of DA (curve d). Although the slope of calibration curve in the modified differential model is only 75.2% of that in the normal photocurrent model (curve a), the LOD is estimated to be 0.018 μM, which is even lower than the previous LOD of 0.031 μM in normal photocurrent signal. The reason is that the differential approach is helpful to suppress the baseline drift of the PEC sensor, resulting in smaller error bars. Table 1 compares the analytical performance of some PEC sensors for DA determination, indicating that the proposed method possesses an acceptable sensitivity.

The reproducibility of the developed PEC biosensor is estimated on the basis of intra-assay and inter-assay relative standard deviations (RSD). The intra-assay RSD is 3.1% in detecting 10.0 μM of DA with five parallel experiments. The RSD of the inter-assay is 4.3% by using five working electrodes fabricated under the identical experimental conditions to incubate with 10.0 μM of DA. Above results reveal that the PEC biosensor possessed a favorable reproducibility and precision. Stability is also critical for the biosensor. After the as-prepared PEC biosensor was stored in a dark and humid environment at 4 °C over 4 weeks, the photocurrent response maintained 86.7% of its original response, demonstrating the excellent stability of the PEC biosensor.

To verify the applicability of the proposed MIPs-based differential PEC method, DA in actual serum samples was determined. In the determination of DA, 0.1 mL of human serum sample was diluted to 10 mL with pH 7 PBS. As listed in Table 2 , DA was not detected in the 100 fold diluted human serum samples. When 10.0 μM DA was added, the recoveries were between 98% and 103%, and the RSD of five measurements was less than 3%.

In this work, we demonstrated that the differential strategy can improve significantly the anti-interference ability of MIPs-based PEC sensor. By using the photocurrent difference between the photoelectrodes with MIPs and NIPs membranes in two independent PEC cells as the signal, the influence of non-specific adsorption of interferents on the outside surface of MIPs membrane on photocurrent can be eliminated effectively, especially in a modified differential model to correct the difference in specific surface area between MIPs and NIPs membranes. For DA determination using MIPs as the recognition element with IF of 4.66, the interference from the same concentration AA or H2O2 were reduced from 31% or 24% in normal PEC model to less than 1% in the differential model. The differential strategy exhibits good stability, repeatability and selectivity, which can be extended to other molecularly imprinting biosensors, including fluorescence, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, electrochemical, and quartz crystal microbalance, etc.

Qi Kang: Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Qiao Zhang: Investigation. Lixin Zang: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Mengru Zhao: Investigation. Xiaolan Chen: Data curation. Dazhong Shen: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874083, 61905132 and 21575080) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province of China (ZR2018MB029).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.05.063.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparison between electrochemical and photoelectrochemical detection of dopamine based on titania-ceria-graphene quantum dots nanocomposite
